question: What is the internal pressure condition of the NuScale containment vessel (CNV) during normal operation?

nuscale_ch05_0012.title: Reactor Coolant Pressure Boundary Leakage Detection
nuscale_ch05_0012.summary: The NuScale reactor coolant pressure boundary (RCPB) leakage detection system does not use traditional components with designed leakage rates, making it impractical to distinguish between identified and unidentified leakage inside containment using installed instrumentation. All leakage into containment is treated as unidentified until its source is determined, with leakage rates monitored via RCS inventory balance and compared to containment evacuation system (CES) flow rates, ensuring reliable detection to support safety analyses.

nuscale_ch05_0024.title: Pressurizer
nuscale_ch05_0024.summary: The pressurizer in the NuScale reactor is integrated into the upper region of the reactor vessel and functions to regulate reactor coolant system (RCS) pressure by maintaining a saturated steam-water interface. It uses heaters and spray flow to control pressure and is designed to handle operating transients without triggering unnecessary reactor trips or safety system actuations. Instrumentation provides continuous monitoring of pressure and water level to support safe operation.

nuscale_ch05_0011.title: Reactor Coolant Pressure Boundary Inservice Inspection and Testing
nuscale_ch05_0011.summary: This section describes the materials and design requirements for threaded fasteners used in key reactor coolant system components, emphasizing the use of Alloy 718 for its compatibility and stress corrosion cracking resistance. It also outlines that preservice and inservice inspection and testing of pressure-retaining components are conducted according to the latest ASME BPVC Section XI standards, as mandated by federal regulations, with inspection intervals and code editions specified in the inspection program.

nuscale_ch05_0004.title: System Description
nuscale_ch05_0004.summary: The Reactor Coolant System (RCS) in the NuScale design is located inside the containment vessel and includes components such as the reactor pressure vessel, pressurizer, and associated piping and controls. The RCS removes heat from the reactor core to the steam generators using natural circulation, and features systems for degasification and emergency core cooling to maintain safety and integrity of the reactor coolant pressure boundary.

nuscale_ch05_0009.title: Overpressure Protection
nuscale_ch05_0009.summary: This section outlines NuScale's compliance with overpressure protection requirements by referencing applicable ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code (BPVC) and Operations and Maintenance (OM) Code cases, as mandated by various sections of 10 CFR 50.55a and related Regulatory Guides (RGs). It details how code cases for design, fabrication, construction, preservice, and inservice inspections and testing are selected and verified to meet NRC-endorsed standards, with a consolidated list provided in Table 5.2-1.

nuscale_ch05_0003.title: Design Basis
nuscale_ch05_0003.summary: The Design Basis section describes the NuScale reactor coolant system (RCS), which uses natural circulation without pumps or external piping to transfer heat from the reactor core to the steam generator. The RCS is designed to ensure safe and efficient cooling, maintain water chemistry, and provide uniform boron concentration under all operating conditions, supporting both performance and safety requirements.

nuscale_ch05_0014.title: Reactor Vessel
nuscale_ch05_0014.summary: The Reactor Vessel section describes the NuScale Power Module's reactor pressure vessel (RPV), which houses the reactor core, steam generators, and pressurizer within a single vessel surrounded by a containment vessel. The RPV serves as a key pressure-retaining component and barrier to fission product release, supporting various reactor systems and constructed according to ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code standards. The materials used for the RPV are carefully selected to ensure integrity and compliance with regulatory requirements throughout the plant's design lifetime.

nuscale_ch05_0017.title: Reactor Vessel Integrity
nuscale_ch05_0017.summary: This section outlines the procedures and regulatory compliance measures for ensuring the integrity of the NuScale reactor vessel. It details the development of operating procedures to maintain pressure-temperature limits, evaluates the vessel’s resistance to pressurized thermal shock and upper-shelf energy degradation over a 60-year design life, and confirms that all criteria remain within regulatory limits.

edges.description: Section B describes the pressurizer's integration with the reactor vessel and its function to rapidly communicate pressure changes with the reactor coolant system (RCS), which is the same system whose leakage and pressure conditions are discussed in Section A, thus both sections provide complementary technical context for understanding the internal pressure condition of the containment vessel during normal operation.

Section A discusses the challenges of detecting reactor coolant pressure boundary leakage inside containment, while Section B details the materials and standards for threaded fasteners used in the pressure boundary, both relating to the integrity and potential leakage sources affecting internal CNV pressure during normal operation.

Section B describes that during normal operation, noncondensible gases are removed from the pressurizer (PZR) volume via a degasification line, indicating that the CNV is not normally exposed to RCS pressure, while Section A discusses leakage detection and the impracticality of distinguishing RCS leakage inside containment, connecting both sections through the context of internal containment conditions during normal operation.

Section A discusses the detection of reactor coolant pressure boundary leakage inside the containment, while Section B outlines the overpressure protection and code compliance for the reactor coolant boundary, connecting them through their focus on maintaining containment integrity and pressure control in accordance with regulatory requirements.

Section A discusses the detection of reactor coolant pressure boundary leakage inside the containment, while Section B describes the design of the reactor coolant system, which determines the internal pressure conditions relevant to leakage detection.

Section A describes the pressurizer's role in maintaining and communicating pressure within the reactor coolant system, which directly determines the internal pressure condition relevant to the containment vessel during normal operation, while Section B discusses the material and design standards for fasteners that ensure the integrity of the pressure boundary under those pressure conditions.

Section B states that during normal operation, noncondensible gases are removed from the pressurizer (PZR) volume at the top of the reactor pressure vessel (RPV) via a degasification line, which implies that the CNV is not directly exposed to PZR steam or pressure under normal conditions, while Section A describes the pressurizer's function and its integration with the RCS, providing context for the pressure conditions managed within the RCS but not directly specifying CNV pressure.

Section A describes the pressurizer's role in maintaining and communicating pressure within the reactor coolant system, which directly relates to Section B's focus on overpressure protection and compliance with regulatory codes to ensure the integrity of the reactor coolant boundary under normal and abnormal pressure conditions.

Section A describes the pressurizer's role in maintaining and communicating pressure within the reactor vessel, while Section B provides the overall design basis of the reactor coolant system, which includes the pressurizer as a component, thus Section A specifies details for the general system described in Section B.

Section A describes the material and design requirements for threaded fasteners in the reactor coolant pressure boundary, while Section B outlines compliance with overpressure protection codes and standards, both of which are connected through their reliance on ASME BPVC requirements for ensuring the integrity and safety of the reactor coolant boundary under pressure conditions.

Section A discusses the materials and inspection requirements for threaded fasteners in the reactor coolant pressure boundary, while Section B provides a general summary of the reactor coolant system design, with both sections connected by their focus on the integrity and configuration of the reactor coolant pressure boundary components.

Section A describes the system features that manage internal pressures in the CNV during normal operation, while Section B outlines the regulatory codes and standards (ASME BPVC, 10 CFR 50.55a) that these pressure management features must comply with, connecting the design implementation to regulatory requirements.

Section A describes specific features of the RCS, such as degasification and venting to the CNV during normal and emergency operations, which provide operational context and mechanisms relevant to understanding the internal pressure condition of the CNV discussed in Section B's general design basis.

Section A references regulatory codes and testing programs that ensure the integrity and overpressure protection of the reactor coolant boundary, while Section B provides a general description of the reactor coolant system's design, which includes the containment vessel whose pressure conditions are governed by the requirements in Section A.

Section A describes the physical arrangement of the reactor and containment vessels, while Section B discusses the detection of reactor coolant pressure boundary leakage within the containment, connecting them through the containment's role in housing and monitoring the reactor system during normal operation.

Section A describes the containment vessel (CNV) as surrounding the reactor pressure vessel (RPV), while Section B explains that the pressurizer, integral to the RPV, rapidly communicates pressure changes within the reactor coolant system, thereby linking the internal pressure conditions of the RPV (and thus the CNV environment) to the pressurizer's operation.

Section A describes the physical arrangement of the containment vessel (CNV) surrounding the reactor pressure vessel (RPV), while Section B details the materials and standards for threaded fasteners used in the RPV and related pressure boundaries, connecting them through their shared focus on pressure-retaining components but not directly addressing CNV internal pressure conditions.

Section A discusses the development of operating procedures to ensure pressure-temperature limits are not exceeded during normal operation, while Section B addresses the detection of leakage from the reactor coolant pressure boundary, both relating to maintaining and monitoring internal pressure conditions within the containment vessel during normal operation.

Section A discusses the development of operating procedures to ensure pressure-temperature limits are not exceeded during normal operation, while Section B describes the pressurizer's role in maintaining and communicating pressure within the reactor vessel, thus both sections are connected through their focus on controlling and maintaining internal pressure conditions during normal operation.

Section A discusses operating procedures to ensure pressure-temperature limits are not exceeded during normal operation, while Section B details the material and design standards for threaded fasteners that must withstand those same pressure conditions, connecting them through the shared requirement to maintain reactor coolant boundary integrity under specified pressures.
